data.title: Rivian Shares Slip on Soft EV Deliveries Forecast
data.description:
By Dean Seal

Shares of Rivian Automotive slipped after the company said it expects to deliver fewer vehicles in 2025 than it did last year against an uncertain backdrop for EV demand.

The stock was down 3.8% at $13.09 in early trading. Shares are now in negative territory year-to-date.

The electric-vehicle maker said after Thursday's closing bell that it projects 46,000 to 51,000 deliveries this year, down from nearly 52,000 in 2024 and beneath the 55,000 that analysts polled by FactSet had expected.

That includes the delivery of 8,000 units in the first quarter, which is below Wall Street targets as the company deals with seasonality and a challenging demand environment, Stifel analysts said in a research note.

Rivian also is facing some near-term uncertainty from a regulatory perspective, particularly when it comes to tariff exposure, that is clouding demand for the year and could lead to higher prices, Benchmark analysts said.

The full-year outlook is relatively conservative as Rivian fights several macroeconomic headwinds, ramps up production and tries to remain prudent about spending on things that can bolster its profitability, Wedbush analysts said in a research note.

Part of the expected deliveries decline is coming from Rivian's planned plant shutdown in the second half of the year, during which it will retool production to support the R2 model that Rivian intends to launch next year, the Wedbush analysts said. The new model is expected to reduce company costs and appeal to a larger market of consumers, they said.

The challenging macroeconomic backdrop is spooking traders more than analysts. Stifel, Wedbush and Benchmark analysts all maintain a buy rating on the stock. They say the company's fourth-quarter performance shows it making steady progress on profitability and that the long-term prospects of the company are strong.

Write to Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com

data.title: Update: i-80 Gold Reports Initial Economic Study Results for Mineral Point Project in Nevada
data.description:
(Adds analyst comment and updates shares.)

i-80 Gold reported Friday the a preliminary economic assessment for the Mineral Point project within the Ruby Hill Complex in Nevada supports construction of a US$707.5-million mine project.

The study outlined a post-tax net present value of US$614 million based on a 5% discount rate, with a 12% internal rate of return.

Average production following ramp-up was estimated at 282,000 ounces per year of gold equivalent over a 16.5-year mine life.

"A key driver of future growth, Mineral Point is the largest of our two planned oxide projects complementing our three high-grade underground mines in northern Nevada," Chief Executive Richard Young said.

The study also included an updated resource estimate. Indicated resources totaled 3.4 million ounces of gold with an average grade of 0.48 grams per tonne (g/t) gold, while inferred resources stood at 2.1 million ounces of gold with average grade of 0.34 g/t gold.

Additionally, the resource hosts 104.3 million ounces of silver at 15.0 g/t in the inferred category and 91.5 million ounces of silver at 14.6 g/t in the indicated category.

"With significant production scale, a long mine life, and low costs, Mineral Point is expected to be the flagship project within our portfolio," Young said.

National Bank of Canada reiterated its outperform rating and $1.75 price target following the release.

Despite first gold pour scheduled in 2031, National Bank said the project represents good optionality on gold prices, and under the right funding and gold price scenario, i-80 could unlock this value.

The company's shares were last seen up $0.03 to $1.17 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: WEC ENERGY GROUP, INC. SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
WEC Energy Group, Inc., a leading energy company with operations in Wisconsin, Michigan, Illinois, and other states, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces. This summary highlights the key aspects of the report, showcasing WEC Energy Group's commitment to transitioning to renewable energy and achieving long-term sustainability goals.

Financial Highlights

  • Operating Revenues: $6,330.5 million, driven by an increase in margins due to the impact of the Wisconsin limited rate case re-openers and a positive impact from collections of fuel and purchased power costs.
  • Gross Margin (GAAP): $2,028.3 million, increased by $77.5 million compared to 2023, driven by rate case re-openers and positive fuel cost collections.
  • Operating Income: $1,575.5 million, increased by $22.2 million compared to 2023, reflecting higher margins and positive impacts from rate adjustments.
  • Net Income Attributed to Common Shareholders: $1,527.2 million, increased by $195.5 million compared to 2023, primarily due to higher net income in the Illinois segment and increased equity earnings from ATC.
  • Diluted Earnings Per Share: $4.83, increased by $0.61 compared to 2023, reflecting overall improved financial performance across segments.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: WEC Energy Group's electric utility operations are divided into Wisconsin, Michigan, and FERC wholesale segments. In 2024, Wisconsin accounted for 91.3% of electric operating revenues, Michigan for 2.9%, and FERC wholesale for 5.8%.
  • Geographical Performance: The Wisconsin segment includes electric and natural gas utility operations, with electric utilities authorized to provide retail service in designated territories in Wisconsin and Michigan. The Illinois segment includes natural gas utility operations in Chicago and northern suburbs, while the other states segment covers Minnesota and Michigan.
  • Sales Units: In 2024, WEC Energy Group served 1,682.7 thousand electric customers and 1,530.9 thousand natural gas customers. The company also reported a 0.7% forecasted increase in retail electric sales volumes for 2025, excluding the Tilden mine.
  • New Product Launches: WEC Energy Group has initiated several renewable energy projects, including the construction of utility-scale solar and wind facilities like Badger Hollow Wind and Whitetail, expected to be completed by 2027.
  • New Production Launches: The company completed the construction of LNG facilities in Wisconsin, providing approximately 2.0 Bcf of natural gas supply. Additional LNG facilities are proposed as part of the 2025-2029 capital plan.
  • Future Outlook: WEC Energy Group plans to invest approximately $9.1 billion from 2025-2029 in regulated renewable energy in Wisconsin, including 2,900 MWs of solar, 900 MWs of wind, and 565 MWs of battery storage. The company aims to achieve net carbon neutrality by 2050.
  • Environmental Goals: The company has set a target to reduce carbon emissions from its electric generation fleet by 60% by 2025 and 80% by 2030, from a 2005 baseline. It plans to achieve these goals through operational refinements and retiring less efficient generating units.
  • Operational Efficiency: WEC Energy Group is advancing its AMI program to replace aging meter-reading equipment, enhancing outage management capabilities and reducing manual efforts for disconnects and reconnects.
  • Reliability Projects: The company is investing in reliability-related projects, including the construction of LNG facilities and the replacement of old iron pipes in Chicago's natural gas delivery system to enhance safety and reliability.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Initiatives: The company is implementing a capital plan focused on transitioning to zero-carbon-emitting renewables and efficient natural gas-fired generation. This includes retiring older fossil-fueled plants and investing approximately $9.1 billion from 2025-2029 in regulated renewable energy in Wisconsin. The plan includes building 2,900 MWs of utility-scale solar, 900 MWs of wind, and 565 MWs of battery storage. Additionally, the company is investing in clean natural gas-fired generation, including 1,100 MWs of combustion turbines at the OCPP site and additional RICE natural gas-fueled generation.
  • Capital Management: The company plans to invest approximately $24.4 billion from 2025 to 2029 in its regulated utility businesses and forecasts $3.2 billion in capital expenditures for its share of ATC's projects over the next five years. In February 2025, the company invested $405.9 million in its non-utility energy infrastructure business with the acquisition of Hardin III. The company also completed the acquisition of an additional 100 MWs of West Riverside's nameplate capacity in May 2024.
  • Future Outlook: The company aims to achieve net carbon neutrality by 2050 and has set goals to reduce carbon emissions from its electric generation fleet by 60% by 2025 and 80% by 2030, from a 2005 baseline. It plans to eliminate coal as an energy source by the end of 2032. The company is also focusing on methane emission reductions with a target of net-zero methane emissions by 2030. Additionally, the company is exploring new technologies and customer preferences to enhance energy resiliency and sustainability.

Challenges and Risks

  • Regulatory Risks: The company faces significant regulatory risks due to extensive governmental oversight, which impacts its ability to recover costs and implement corporate strategies. Changes in regulations or interpretations could require operational changes and incur substantial compliance costs. Recent regulatory decisions in Illinois have led to disallowed capital costs and impairment losses, affecting future capital plans and cost recovery.
  • Environmental Compliance Costs: Environmental compliance costs are substantial, with evolving laws and regulations potentially increasing operational costs and impacting the viability of coal-fired facilities. The company plans to retire additional coal-fired generation and invest in renewable energy to meet carbon reduction goals, but these efforts depend on external factors like technology development and regulatory support.
  • Transition to Lower GHG Emissions: The transition to lower GHG emissions presents policy, legal, and market risks, potentially requiring significant capital expenditures. Electrification initiatives and mandates could increase electrical demand and investment costs, while reducing natural gas demand.
  • Cybersecurity Threats: Cybersecurity threats pose operational risks, with potential disruptions to critical infrastructure and data breaches. The company is investing in security measures but acknowledges the increasing sophistication of cyber threats.
  • Supply Chain Disruptions and Inflation: Supply chain disruptions and inflation are impacting project costs and timelines, particularly for renewable energy projects. Geopolitical tensions and trade policies could exacerbate these issues, affecting the availability and cost of materials and equipment.
  • Market Risks: The company is exposed to market risks related to commodity price fluctuations, particularly for natural gas and coal. These fluctuations can impact working capital requirements and profitability. The company uses derivative instruments to manage these risks but acknowledges potential financial losses if market conditions change unexpectedly.

SEC Filing: WEC ENERGY GROUP, INC. [ WEC ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: SYNOVUS FINANCIAL CORP SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Synovus Financial Corp, a financial services company and registered bank holding company headquartered in Columbus, Georgia, has released its 2024 10-K report. The report provides a comprehensive overview of the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the competitive financial services industry.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $1,989.2 million, down 10% from 2023, primarily due to realized net losses from sales of AFS investment securities.
  • Net Income: $482.5 million, down 11% from 2023, impacted by strategic repositioning of the investment securities portfolio.
  • Net Income Available to Common Shareholders: $439.6 million, down 13% from 2023, reflecting lower non-interest expense and lower provision for credit losses.
  • Net Income Per Common Share, Diluted: $3.03, down 12% from 2023, influenced by lower revenue and strategic repositioning.
  • Net Interest Income: $1,749.6 million, down 4% from 2023, as increased funding costs more than offset the benefits of higher asset yields.
  • Net Interest Margin: 3.19%, slightly down from 3.21% in 2023, due to increased funding costs.
  • Non-interest Revenue: $239.6 million, down 41% from 2023, negatively impacted by losses from sales of AFS investment securities.
  • Non-interest Expense: $1,247.5 million, a decrease of 7% from 2023, due to lower accrual related to the FDIC special assessment and restructuring charges.
  • Provision for Credit Losses: $136.7 million, lower than 2023, driven by decreased net charge-offs and lower loan balances.

Business Highlights

  • Business Overview: Synovus Financial Corp. operates through its wholly-owned subsidiary, Synovus Bank, offering a range of services including commercial and consumer banking, wealth services, treasury management, and mortgage services across Alabama, Florida, Georgia, South Carolina, and Tennessee.
  • Banking Operations: Synovus Bank provides commercial and consumer banking services through 244 branches and 357 ATMs, offering services such as commercial, financial, and real estate lending, treasury management, asset management, and consumer banking services including deposit accounts, loans, and card services.
  • Non-bank Subsidiaries: Synovus Securities specializes in portfolio management, investment banking, and financial planning, while Synovus Trust provides trust and asset management services.
  • Strategic Focus: In 2024, Synovus focused on expanding and diversifying its franchise, enhancing profitability, deepening relationships, and investing in technology and digital capabilities. The strategic plan for 2025 emphasizes accelerated growth investments, particularly in middle market banking, specialty lending, and wealth services.
  • Human Capital Resources: Synovus emphasizes relationship banking and aims to attract and retain a skilled workforce. As of December 31, 2024, Synovus employed 4,775 people, with a focus on talent acquisition, development, and retention, and maintaining a diverse and inclusive workforce.
  • Regulatory Environment: Synovus is subject to extensive federal and state regulation, impacting its operations and strategic decisions. The company is a financial holding company under the Federal Reserve and must comply with various regulatory requirements, including capital adequacy standards and consumer protection laws.
  • Future Outlook: For 2025, Synovus plans to focus on growth in middle market banking, specialty lending, and wealth services, with initiatives to enhance product development and client penetration in treasury and payment solutions. The company also aims to continue investing in infrastructure to improve client experience and operational efficiency.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Repositioning: Synovus undertook a strategic repositioning of its investment securities portfolio in 2024, selling $1.62 billion of mortgage-backed securities and purchasing $1.48 billion in new securities, including U.S. Treasury and commercial mortgage-backed securities. This repositioning aimed to reduce exposure to AOCI volatility and optimize the portfolio's yield. Additionally, Synovus transferred $2.72 billion of securities from AFS to HTM to further stabilize its investment portfolio.
  • Capital Management: In 2024, Synovus repurchased 6.4 million shares of common stock at an average price of $42.40 per share, as part of a $300 million share repurchase authorization. The company also issued $500 million of 6.168% Fixed Rate/Floating Rate Senior Notes maturing in 2030 to strengthen its capital position. Synovus maintained a CET1 ratio of 10.84%, exceeding regulatory requirements, and paid $450 million in dividends from Synovus Bank to the Parent Company. The Board approved a capital plan for 2025, including a quarterly dividend of $0.39 per share and authorized share repurchases of up to $400 million of common stock and $50 million of preferred stock.
  • Future Outlook: For 2025, Synovus plans to maintain a stable CET1 ratio and leverage share repurchases to balance organic capital generation. The company anticipates end-of-period loan growth of 3% to 6% and core deposit growth of 3% to 6%, with adjusted revenue and non-interest expense growth of 3% to 7%. Synovus will continue to focus on strategic capital management to support its growth objectives and maintain robust liquidity across diverse sources.

Challenges and Risks

  • Competitive Landscape: Synovus operates in a highly competitive financial services industry, facing challenges from both traditional banks and non-bank financial institutions, including fintech companies. The company is focused on expanding its digital capabilities and enhancing its product offerings to remain competitive. However, the rapid technological changes and the need for significant investments in technology pose operational challenges.
  • Strategic Risks: Synovus faces several strategic risks, including intense competition in the financial services industry, which may impact future earnings and growth. The company must adapt to technological advancements and changing client expectations, which require substantial investments. There is also a risk of not realizing the expected benefits from strategic initiatives, which could negatively impact profitability.
  • Operational Risks: Attracting and retaining qualified employees is critical for executing growth strategies. The company is also exposed to significant cyber and data security risks, which could result in reputational harm and financial liabilities.
  • Regulatory Risks: Regulatory risks are heightened by potential changes in the regulatory landscape, which could increase compliance costs and impact business operations. Additionally, the use of artificial intelligence presents new challenges, including regulatory uncertainties and the risk of biased or incorrect outputs.
  • Market Risks: Synovus is exposed to market risks, including interest rate fluctuations, which can affect net interest income and the value of financial assets. The company employs hedging and other risk mitigation strategies to manage these risks, but there is no guarantee of their effectiveness. The economic environment, including inflationary pressures and geopolitical tensions, adds to the uncertainty and potential volatility in financial markets, which could adversely affect the company's operations and financial condition.

SEC Filing: SYNOVUS FINANCIAL CORP [ SNV ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: Stellantis' Lawsuit Over Cartel Activity in UK Dismissed by Competition Appeal Tribunal
data.description:
Stellantis' lawsuit against a group of automotive safety equipment makers, including Autoliv (ALV), was dismissed by the UK's Competition Appeal Tribunal for lack of evidence, according to a Friday statement from the Tribunal.

In its complaint, Stellantis alleged that the group was involved in cartels related to markets for seatbelts, airbags and steering wheels from 2002 or 2004 through 2011, leading to an overcharge of 10.8% to 25.9% on the products over the period.

UK's Competition Appeal Tribunal said Stellantis "failed to establish" that there was cartel activity over the period or that any cartel activity led to an overcharge.

Stellantis and Autoliv did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' requests for comments.

Dye & Durham Appoints Second Interim CEO in Three Months — Market Talk
data.description: Dye & Durham's new management names its second interim CEO since the board overthrow in December. After the activist investor Engine Capital successfully had its candidates appointed to the board at the end of last year, the Canadian cloud-based platform named Hans Gieskes the new chairman and interim chief executive. On Friday, Gieskes' roles were removed and distributed to two other candidates, Sid Singh taking over as the new interim chief executive, effective immediately and Engine founder and portfolio manager Arnaud Ajdler as chair. Gieskes will remain on the board. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: SANOMA CORPORATION: ACQUISITION OF OWN SHARES 21 February 2025
Kellanova Declares Regular Dividend of $0.57 per Share
data.description:
Kellanova Declares Regular Dividend of $0.57 per Share

PR Newswire

CHICAGO, Feb. 21, 2025

CHICAGO, Feb. 21, 2025 /PRNewswire/ — Kellanova today announced that its Board of Directors declared a dividend of $0.57 per share on the common stock of Kellanova, payable on March 14, 2025, to shareowners of record at the close of business on March 3, 2025. The ex-dividend date is March 3, 2025. This is the 401(st) dividend that Kellanova, previously Kellogg Company, has paid to owners of common stock since 1925.

About Kellanova

Kellanova is a leader in global snacking, international cereal and noodles, and North America frozen foods with a legacy stretching back more than 100 years. Powered by differentiated brands including Pringles(R) , Cheez-It(R) , Pop-Tarts(R) , Kellogg's (R) Rice Krispies Treats(R) , RXBAR(R) , Eggo(R) , MorningStar Farms(R) , Special K(R) , Coco Pops(R) , and more, Kellanova's vision is to become the world's best-performing snacks-led powerhouse, unleashing the full potential of our differentiated brands and our passionate people. Our net sales for 2024 were approximately $13 billion.

At Kellanova, our purpose is to create better days and ensure everyone has a seat at the table through our trusted food brands. We are committed to promoting sustainable and equitable food access by tackling the crossroads of hunger, sustainability, wellbeing, and equity, diversity & inclusion. Our goal is to create Better Days for 4 billion people by the end of 2030 (from a 2015 baseline). For more detailed information about our commitments, our approach to achieving these goals, and methodology, please visit our website at https://www.kellanova.com.

[K-DIV] [K-FIN]

View original content to download multimedia:https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/kellanova-declares-regular-dividend-of-0-57-per-share-302382411.html

SOURCE Kellanova IR

data.title: DOJ Investigates Medicare Billing Practices at UnitedHealth — WSJ
data.description:
By Christopher Weaver and Anna Wilde Mathews

The Justice Department has launched an investigation into UnitedHealth Group's Medicare billing practices in recent months, people familiar with the matter say.

The new civil fraud investigation is examining the company's practices for recording diagnoses that trigger extra payments to its Medicare Advantage plans, including at physician groups the insurance giant owns.

A series of articles in The Wall Street Journal last year showed that Medicare paid UnitedHealth billions of dollars for questionable diagnoses. Attorneys with the Justice Department as recently as Jan. 31 interviewed medical providers named in the articles.

In the Medicare Advantage system, insurers get lump-sum payments from the federal government to oversee enrollees' Medicare benefits. When patients have certain diagnoses, the payments go up, creating an incentive to diagnose more diseases.

The Medicare billing investigation adds to the scrutiny on UnitedHealth, the $400 billion company that owns the largest U.S. health insurer and a sprawling network of other health-industry assets including its doctor practices, a large pharmacy-benefit manager and data and technology operations.

The civil investigation, some of the people said, is separate from a longer-running Justice Department antitrust probe that the Journal reported last February. The DOJ also has sued to block UnitedHealth's $3.3 billion planned acquisition of home-health company Amedisys on antitrust grounds.

A spokesman for the Justice Department declined to comment. A spokeswoman for the Department of Health and Human Services' Office of Inspector General, which is also involved in the civil fraud probe, declined to comment.

UnitedHealth declined to comment. UnitedHealth previously has said its practices lead to more accurate diagnoses, that it performs well in Medicare audits and that its approach benefits patients.

In December, the Journal reported that its analysis of billions of Medicare records showed that patients examined by UnitedHealth-employed doctors had huge increases in lucrative diagnoses after joining the company's Medicare Advantage plans.

Doctors said UnitedHealth, based in the Minneapolis area, trained them to document revenue-generating diagnoses, including some they felt were obscure or irrelevant. The company also used software to suggest conditions and paid bonuses for considering the suggestions, among other tactics, according to the doctors.

Last summer, the Journal also reported that UnitedHealth added diagnoses to patients' records for conditions that no doctor treated, which triggered an extra $8.7 billion in federal payments in 2021. The untreated diagnoses stemmed from sources including in-home visits by nurses working for the company's HouseCalls unit. Each visit by the UnitedHealth-employed nurses was worth an average of $2,735 in additional federal payments, a Journal analysis of Medicare data spanning 2019 to 2021 found.

Last month, Justice Department lawyers from the offices of the U.S. Attorney for Minnesota and the Washington, D.C.-based Civil Division contacted at least three doctors and a nurse practitioner who were named in the Journal's story on UnitedHealth-owned clinics. One of the people was told the health department's Office of Inspector General was involved as well.

Three said they were questioned about specific diagnoses UnitedHealth promoted for employees to use with patients, incentive arrangements and pressure to add the diagnoses. At least two provided documents, including a contract with a UnitedHealth unit, to the Justice Department.

Valerie O'Meara, a nurse practitioner who worked for UnitedHealth in Washington state, said she was interviewed on Jan. 31 by Justice Department attorneys who were interested in the company software that suggested diagnoses and the role of a UnitedHealth manager who she said urged her to make new diagnoses beyond what doctors had treated.

The attorneys zeroed in on certain diagnoses the company often suggested, such as an obscure hormonal condition called secondary hyperaldosteronism, she said. The Journal's analysis found the condition was rarely diagnosed by Medicare doctors not working for UnitedHealth.

O'Meara said the attorneys focused on her account of how she was told she could add the hyperaldosteronism diagnosis to patients' records without a lab test.

More broadly, she said, "they were looking at, 'Is this abuse?' "

UnitedHealth has said its practices help detect diseases earlier, saving money for the health system. The company says Medicare Advantage plans generate better health outcomes and reduce costs.

In a press release published on its website on Dec. 30, UnitedHealth said the Journal's articles "rely on often incomplete and inaccurate data to conduct flawed studies through a murky government 'agreement'."

The Journal's analyses used data UnitedHealth and other Medicare Advantage insurers themselves submitted to the federal government for payment purposes. Reporters accessed the data under a standard research agreement with the agency overseeing Medicare.

UnitedHealth has faced investigations of its diagnosis-documenting practices before, and its Medicare payments have been examined in health department inspector general reports. The Justice Department took over an earlier lawsuit by a former UnitedHealth employee alleging the company failed to retract inaccurate diagnoses added to patients' records.

UnitedHealth has disputed the allegations against it in that continuing case.

Write to Christopher Weaver at Christopher.Weaver@wsj.com and Anna Wilde Mathews at Anna.Mathews@wsj.com

data.title: Week Ahead - Feb 24th
data.description:
Next week, investors will focus on speeches by several Federal Reserve officials in the United States, alongside key economic data, including personal income and spending, PCE price indices, durable goods orders, the second estimate of Q4 GDP growth, and CB consumer sentiment.

In the housing sector, attention will be on the S&P/Case-Shiller Home Price YoY data, as well as new and pending home sales.

Globally, inflation figures will be released for France, Italy, Germany, Spain, South Africa, and Australia.

GDP growth rates are expected from Switzerland, Turkey, Brazil, India, and Canada.

Meanwhile, Germany will publish its Ifo Business Climate Index, GfK consumer confidence, and retail sales figures.

data.title: Private-Sector Output Growth Unexpectedly Slows, Optimism Wanes, S&P Survey Shows
data.description:
Output growth in the US private sector unexpectedly slowed in February as the services sector contracted, while optimism waned amid worries over the impact of tariffs and domestic spending cuts, according to S&P Global's flash purchasing managers' index released Friday.

The composite output index fell to a 17-month low of 50.4 this month from 52.7 in January, compared with the 53.2 consensus in a survey compiled by Bloomberg. The 50-point mark separates expansion from contraction.

Services sector output decreased to 49.7 from 52.9, marking the first contraction in 25 months. The manufacturing PMI hit an eight-month high of 51.6 from 51.2 last month. Wall Street was looking for 53 and 51.4, respectively.

"The upbeat mood seen among US businesses at the start of the year has evaporated, replaced with a darkening picture of heightened uncertainty, stalling business activity and rising prices," S&P Global Market Intelligence Chief Business Economist Chris Williamson said.

Business optimism about the coming year fell to its lowest level since December 2022, except for last September.

"Companies report widespread concerns about the impact of federal government policies, ranging from spending cuts to tariffs and geopolitical developments," Williamson said.

New business inflows into the services sector nearly stagnated as service providers cited uncertainty related to spending cuts' potential impact on economic growth and inflation outlooks.

"A concern is the sharp, tariff-related, jump in manufacturing input prices, which will likely either put further upward pressure on inflation in the coming months or further squeeze profit margins among US companies," Williamson added.

Manufacturing output grew for the second straight month in February, buoyed by an increase in new orders.

Consolidated employment fell for the first time in three months.

data.title: Bernstein Adjusts Gilead Sciences Price Target to $120 From $105

data.title: Update: China's Pony AI Expands Paid Robotaxi Services in Guangzhou; Shares Rise
data.description:
(Updates with the latest stock price movement in the headline and last paragraph.)

Pony AI said Friday it introduced paid robotaxi services from multiple locations in Guangzhou's city center to Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport and Guangzhou South Railway Station in China.

The move comes after the autonomous mobility company introduced paid robotaxi rides to and from Beijing Daxing International Airport and to and from Beijing South Railway Station.

The company's US-listed shares were up more than 20% in recent trading.

data.title: Thames Water Utilities: It Also Comprises Capacity for a Further GBP1.5B Across Two Tranches of GBP750M
Hasbro Finds Magic Again With Transformation, Morgan Stanley Says
data.description:
Hasbro enters 2025 with momentum as its turnaround efforts start to pay off, with strong Q4 performance capping off an impressive year of profitability improvement, Morgan Stanley said in a Thursday note.

The report said Hasbro delivered 1,000 bps of operating margin expansion, eclipsing the 20% threshold well ahead of its original

plan, and positioning it to shift to the offense.

Morgan Stanley also pointed to visibility to continued upward revisions as the team proceeds to simplify the business, cut costs, invest in innovation, and drive core topline growth.

"HAS's earnings and cash flow potential remains underappreciated by the market today and it remains a preferred pick in our leisure coverage," the note said.

Morgan Stanley lowered its price target to $84 from $88, while maintaining an overweight rating.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1118 ET - News that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration declared the shortage of Novo Nordisk's weight-loss and diabetes drugs Wegovy and Ozempic over marks an important step toward a normalized obesity-drug market in the U.S., Barclays analysts say in a note. The move should eventually lead to pharmacies or physicians no longer producing copies of the branded drugs, Barclays says. Nevertheless, the exact date when copycat versions of the drugs come off market remains somewhat uncertain, the analysts say. The FDA designation shows the Danish pharmaceutical company's efforts to scale up production have reached a level that it can now supply the market for type 2 diabetes and obesity, Barclays says. Shares rise 5.8%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

1054 ET - Sika expects a difficult year for its automotive business in Europe, CEO Thomas Hasler says in an interview. The Switzerland-based specialty-chemical company is running at the output level for cars in Europe, which is far below the usual going rate, indicating a huge backlog, Hasler says. "Consumers are hesitant to invest. The electrification path is somehow stalled and incentives are not in place to stimulate," he says. However, the fleet in Europe is aging drastically and will have to recover in the years to come, Hasler says. This year is probably still going to see declining volumes, also given the geopolitical uncertainty, he adds. Shares trade 0.5% higher at 236.70 Swiss francs. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

1043 ET - Sika's integration of MBCC Group is well on track, CEO Thomas Hasler says in an interview. The Switzerland-based specialty-chemical company acquired the construction chemicals supplier in 2023 and has generated synergies beyond the original guidance, the CEO says. "It's fantastic to see how it contributes on all levels on the top line, but also on the others we have been able to almost equalize the original or initial dilution of the business," Hasler says. The CEO is very positive about the future, he says, adding that no job cuts are planned. Shares trade 0.5% higher at 236.70 Swiss francs. (nina.kienle@wsj.com)

1014 ET - A new Redfin report shows that half of the nation's 10 priciest home listings are in coastal Florida, with three in Southern California, one in New York City, and one in Lake Tahoe. They all exceed $100 million, with two surpassing $200 million. Despite Florida's rising natural disaster risks and insurance costs, its luxurious beachfront lifestyle, warm climate, and no state income tax draw wealthy buyers. Towns like Miami Beach and Naples boast extravagant estates for the ultra-rich. Los Angeles and New York also attract millionaires and billionaires. Last month's top sales included three in coastal Florida, three in Manhattan, two in Los Angeles, and two in Aspen. The highest was a $53.5 million 5th Avenue apartment in New York, followed by a $50.5 million Delray Beach, FL, estate. (chris.wack@wsj.com)

1005 ET - UnitedHealth Group is sharply lower after WSJ reports that the Department of Justice is investigating the company's Medicare billing practices. Mizuho analysts say in a report that while the investigation is new, the issues brought into question aren't. They don't believe there will be an incremental negative impact to earnings from the probe. "We would be buyers of UNH on the weakness," the analysts say. UnitedHealth sinks 9%. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0944 ET - Chorus Aviation's Voyageur business is showing promising growth, and TD Cowen's Tim James thinks this should help the stock take off. In a report, the analyst says Chorus' specialized aviation services segment is gaining momentum, and should reach its 2025 revenue target of C$150 million "driven by part sales, contract wins and expansion of its existing contracts in the defence surveillance verticals." TD Cowen upgrades the stock to buy from hold and raises the target price to C$27 from C$25. Shares are currently about 12% lower year-to-date at C$19.26. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0943 ET - Oil prices are forecast to fall further in the coming months even if OPEC+ decides to delay its planned output hike, according to Capital Economics. The cartel and its allies are set to start raising output from early April, but market watchers widely expect the group to postpone its plans once again amid broad uncertainties around the global economic and geopolitical outlook. Meanwhile, global demand remains subdued despite a slight pick up in the fourth quarter of last year and non-OPEC+ growth is expected to remain robust. "OPEC+'s dilemma isn't set to get any easier this year," says economist Kieran Tompkins. "A further delay would be reflective of weak fundamentals in the oil market." (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

0921 ET - Air Liquide's 2024 results were in line with consensus but its higher margin outlook indicates that its margin opportunity is currently underappreciated by investors, Citi analysts say in a note. The French industrial-gases company targets a margin increase of 200 basis points over two years, ahead of the 140 basis points expected by a Visible Alpha consensus. Its underlying growth remains relatively low but it seems that management is optimistic of an acceleration in 2025 based on electronics and less turnarounds, the analysts say. Shares are up 3.6% at 179.00 euros. (helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0855 ET - With potash appearing to make a comeback, Nutrien is positioned to benefit. In a Raymond James report, Steve Hansen says he sees tangible signs of life in the global potash markets, including solid demand and rising prices across most global regions. Nutrien management also sees pricing momentum continuing in 2025, Hansen says, thanks to strong grower affordability as crop prices rise, strong demand from Brazil and Southeast Asia, and balanced inventory levels across key markets such as China, India and Southeast Asia. Hansen says that other key providers in Laos and Russia are currently facing operational challenges and maintenance outages. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

0834 ET - U.S. Cellular is still seeing a drop in subscribers but logged a meaningful improvement in prepaid and postpaid customer churn during 4Q as it works to close an acquisition by its larger telecom rival T-Mobile. The Chicago company, which caters to mostly rural customers, closed 2024 with almost 3.99 million postpaid connections, in which service is paid for each month on a contract. That's down from 4.11 million at the end of 2023. But the postpaid churn rate was just 1.29%, compared with 1.44% a year ago, and prepaid churn was 3.7%, stacked against 3.87% in the year-ago quarter. Shares rise 3% premarket. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0804 ET - The outcome of Germany's federal election could trigger an explosive rally for the country's stock market and fuel a broader European equity surge, Nigel Green, the CEO of financial advisory company deVere Group, writes. Should front-runner Friedrich Merz secure a coalition with the Social Democratic Party and potentially the Greens, it could usher in a wave of economic reforms and a possible relaxation of the debt brake, he writes. This could set the stage for a sustained bull run, Green adds. European equities have been hindered by economic stagnation and political uncertainty. However, the headwinds are diminishing, and a decisive result in the German election opens the door for potentially sustained outperformance, he adds. The DAX trades up 0.2% at 22360.40. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0717 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.2% at $9,535.50 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 1.3% at $2,695 a ton. However, the base metals complex is set to end the week higher on mounting concerns of trade wars and a generally weaker U.S. dollar, with copper up 0.7% on week and aluminum up 2.3% on week. Among North American copper miners, 2024 copper production was on average 5% below original guidance, and 2025 guidance was 5% below consensus estimates, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. This likely reflects more conservatism on guidance from miners and more challenging mining. Some recent positive signs around U.S.-China relations are constructive for metals and the fundamentals for copper look solid, though uncertainty remains short-term, RBC adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

data.title: PITNEY BOWES INC /DE/ SEC 10-K Report
data.description:
Pitney Bowes Inc., a global technology company that provides innovative products and solutions in the areas of mailing, shipping, and financial services, has released its 2024 Form 10-K report. The report outlines the company's financial performance, business operations, strategic initiatives, and the challenges it faces in the current market environment.

Financial Highlights

  • Total Revenue: $2,026.6 million, a decrease of 3% from 2023, primarily due to lower support services and equipment sales, partially offset by higher business services revenue.
  • Gross Margin: $855.4 million, representing a gross margin percentage of 66.8%, an increase from 65.8% in 2023, driven by improvements in business services and cost management.
  • Loss from Continuing Operations Before Income Taxes: $(52.3) million, reflecting a 19% increase in loss compared to 2023.
  • Net Income (Loss) from Continuing Operations: $102.5 million, a significant improvement from a loss of $61.3 million in 2023, primarily due to a tax benefit from an affiliate reorganization.
  • Net Loss: $(203.6) million, improved by 47% from a net loss of $385.6 million in 2023, including a net loss from discontinued operations of $306.1 million.

Business Highlights

  • Business Segments: Pitney Bowes operates through two main business segments: SendTech Solutions and Presort Services. SendTech Solutions offers physical and digital shipping and mailing technology solutions, while Presort Services provides mail sortation services to qualify large volumes of mail for postal discounts.
  • Revenue Segments: Within SendTech Solutions, business services revenue increased by 31% in 2024, driven by growth in shipping subscriptions and digital delivery services. However, support services and equipment sales saw declines of 9% and 11%, respectively, due to a shift towards lease extensions and a declining meter population.
  • Geographical Performance: In 2024, 16% of Pitney Bowes' consolidated revenue was generated from operations outside the United States, indicating a significant portion of business activities occurring internationally.
  • Sales Units: The company experienced a decline in equipment sales as customers opted to extend leases of existing equipment rather than purchase new units, reflecting a shift in customer preferences.
  • New Product Launches: The company has been focusing on enhancing its SaaS solutions within the SendTech Solutions segment, aiming to drive growth in shipping offerings.
  • Future Outlook: Pitney Bowes anticipates a decline in mailing-related revenues due to lower meter populations and a higher mix of lease extensions. However, growth is expected in shipping offerings, particularly SaaS solutions. Presort Services is projected to see revenue and margin improvements due to investments in automation and technology.
  • Operational Efficiency: The company has implemented cost rationalization and cash optimization strategies, resulting in lower costs and improved productivity across its operations.
  • Regulatory Compliance: Pitney Bowes' operations are subject to various regulations, including postal authority regulations, transportation regulations, and data privacy and security laws, which impact its business operations globally.

Strategic Initiatives

  • Strategic Transformation: The company undertook a strategic transformation focusing on four key initiatives: Ecommerce Restructuring, cost rationalization, cash optimization, and balance sheet deleveraging. The Ecommerce Restructuring involved the wind-down of the Global Ecommerce segment, with Hilco acquiring 81% of the voting interests in DRF Logistics, LLC. The company retained 19% of the voting interests and 100% of the economic interests. This restructuring included a Chapter 11 bankruptcy filing for the Ecommerce Debtors and a Restructuring Support Agreement to facilitate an orderly wind-down.
  • Capital Management: The company managed its capital by repaying $178 million of Notes due March 2028 and making scheduled principal repayments of $56 million. A new senior secured credit agreement was entered into in February 2025, providing a $265 million revolving credit facility and term loans totaling $775 million. The company also authorized a new $150 million share repurchase program and increased its quarterly dividend to $0.06 per share. Additionally, the company provided a $47 million DIP Facility to the Ecommerce Debtors, with an initial funding of $28 million and a partial repayment of $11 million.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to continue its strategic focus on cost reduction and cash optimization. Within SendTech Solutions, the company expects a decline in mailing-related revenues but anticipates growth in shipping offerings, particularly SaaS solutions. Presort Services is expected to see revenue and margin improvements due to pricing actions and investments in automation. The company aims to maintain adequate liquidity through cash generated from operations and borrowing capacity, with a focus on sustainable growth and capital investment opportunities.

Challenges and Risks

  • Mailing and Shipping Industry Risks: The financial condition of the USPS and other national posts could adversely affect client demand and financial performance. Changes in postal regulations and a decline in physical mail volumes pose additional risks.
  • Business Operational Risks: The company faces intense competition, supply chain disruptions, and challenges in managing third-party suppliers. Labor shortages and increased transportation costs could impact operations.
  • Cybersecurity and Technology Risks: The company is vulnerable to cyberattacks, which could disrupt operations and lead to legal liabilities. Compliance with data privacy laws is also a concern.
  • Macroeconomic and General Regulatory Risks: Economic conditions, changes in tax laws, and trade policies could adversely affect the business. The company is also exposed to risks related to ESG expectations and shareholder activism.
  • Market Risks: Pitney Bowes is exposed to market risks from foreign currency exchange rates and interest rates. The company manages these risks through derivatives and is also exposed to credit risk on its receivables. A 1% change in foreign currency exchange rates could significantly impact earnings, and a 100 basis point change in interest rates would affect interest expense.

SEC Filing: PITNEY BOWES INC /DE/ [ PBI ] - 10-K - Feb. 21, 2025

data.title: Update: Emera Q4 Adjusted Earnings Beat Estimates
data.description:
(Adds comment and updates shares.)

Emera on Friday reported fourth-quarter adjusted earnings that beat estimates.

The utility-holding company posted adjusted earnings, excluding most one-time items, of $246 million, or $0.84 per share, beating the FactSet analyst consensus forecast of $0.76 per share and above the $175 million, or $0.63, recorded in the year-prior quarter.

Revenue was not disclosed.

Emera attributed the higher adjusted earnings to increased income across all of its regulated utilities, as well as higher corporate income tax recovery.

These were partially offset by lower equity earnings due to the sale of the Labrador Island Link in the second quarter of 2024, higher corporate operating, maintenance and general expense due to the timing of long-term incentive expenses and related hedges, increased Corporate interest expense, and decreased earnings at Emera Energy.

Net income was $154 million, or $0.52 per share, below the FactSet analyst consensus estimate of $0.77 and lower than the year-ago pr of $289 million, or $1.04 per share.

"2024 was a year of significant progress for Emera," chief executive Scott Balfour said. "We delivered on our key objectives and in doing so, have strategically positioned the business to meet the evolving demands within our industry and capitalize on the robust organic growth opportunities in our Florida utilities."

For 2025, Emera said it will focus on executing its five-year $20 billion capital plan, grid modernization and infrastructure expansion, as well as growing earnings and cash flow.

National Bank of Canada maintained its sector-perform rating and $56.00 price target on Emera shares following the results.

"With a solid finish to 2024, and the company's five-year capital plan in check, we expect modest changes to our long-term estimates and valuation following the company's conference call," National Bank said.

Emera shares were last seen up $0.28 to $56.89 on the Toronto Stock Exchange.

data.title: Market Chatter: Mastercard's Settlement of Card Fees Lawsuit Approved by UK Tribunal
data.description:
Mastercard's settlement of a lawsuit over card fees was approved by the UK Competition Appeal Tribunal, Reuters reported Friday.

The tribunal approved an agreement announced in December 2024 to settle the case for 200 million pounds ($252.8 million), according to the report.

Innsworth Capital, which funded the litigation, had argued that the settlement should not be approved, the report said. It did not respond to Reuters' request for comment.

Mastercard and Innsworth did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: Should Broadcom really buy parts of Intel? It could be 'extremely lucrative.'
data.description:
By Therese Poletti

Broadcom would only be interested in Intel's core PC- and server-chip units, Piper Sandler speculates

As Wall Street continues to speculate about a potential split of chip giant Intel Corp., Piper Sandler believes that a Broadcom Inc. acquisition of the company's core x86 business "would be extremely lucrative."

Last weekend, the Wall Street Journal added another dimension to the slew of reports on Intel's (INTC) future, reporting that Broadcom (AVGO) has been closely examining Intel's chip-design and -marketing business as part of a possible deal that would split Intel into two. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co. (TW:2330) has studied controlling some or all of Intel's chip plants, possibly as part of an investor consortium, the Journal also reported.

"Assuming that [Broadcom] management pays a premium to the current market cap and is able to turn this business around just as it has turned around almost every other business that it has previously acquired, we think that the numbers become extremely compelling," Piper Sandler analyst Harsh Kumar wrote in a note to clients.

Kumar said he arrived at a potential sale price of $101 billion, with a ratio of about 3.47x on the basis of debt to earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization. He described those numbers as "very palatable in the world of semis." Intel could add about $1.50 to $1.60 in earnings per share to Broadcom, he added.

The deal could be fully financed by debt, and Broadcom could scale down Intel's client business, which makes personal-computer chips, "by pruning" businesses, Kumar wrote. "Similar to previous deals, we believe [Broadcom] would strategically exit certain segments of the client market." That could mean the segment would earn 25% less revenue.

It was not clear if Kumar's projected price would include the cost of renegotiating a cross-licensing pact with Advanced Micro Devices Inc. (AMD). Since 2009, Intel and AMD have had in place a cross-licensing agreement for x86 technologies that would terminate upon a change in control of either company. Kumar did not immediately respond to a request for clarification.

He also believes that Broadcom would shut down Intel's artificial-intelligence chip business, which currently develops the Gaudi chip. Intel has already canceled its next-generation AI chip, which was code-named Falcon Shores.

On Feb. 11, Intel's stock began a major run after Vice President J.D. Vance made positive comments about the Trump administration's plans to boost the semiconductor industry in the United States. Around that time, reports also emerged that the administration was trying to broker a deal for Intel's manufacturing business, including one that would put TSMC in charge of the operations.

Also read: Intel's stock-market run is unlike anything it's seen in more than two decades

Kumar said he is convinced that Broadcom is not interested at all in Intel's manufacturing operations, which have been separated from the chip-design business where its core PC and server chips reside. He also said that he believes Broadcom would probably sell off Intel's networking and edge business, since that is not in a leading position and does not fit Broadcom's strategy.

Over the past 10 years, Broadcom has grown into a massive tech conglomerate through a series of acquisitions, often of struggling tech companies that were once leaders in their fields. In fiscal 2024, Broadcom's total revenue was $51.6 billion, up from $6.8 billion in fiscal 2015. The deals started that year when Avago Technologies, which had its roots as a semiconductor unit of Hewlett Packard Co., bought Broadcom Communications, a Southern California-based chip designer, for $37 billion.

Some of Broadcom's biggest deals have included software maker VMware, CA Technologies (formerly Computer Associates) and Symantec.

Kumar reiterated an overweight rating on Broadcom. In morning trading, Broadcom's stock was off fractionally, while Intel's was down nearly 2%.

-Therese Poletti

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: ICE Canola Weakens, Crude Oil Sinks
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--The ICE Futures canola market showed small declines in the middle of trading on Friday due to mostly negative sentiment in comparable oils.

Malaysian palm oil was positive, but Chicago soyoil and European rapeseed were lower. Crude oil lost more than US$1 per barrel due to rising U.S. stockpiles and refinery maintenance. This is despite a deterioration in peace talks between Russia and Ukraine and a Ukrainian drone strike on a Russian pumping station.

One analyst said he is becoming hesitant to put more funds into canola due to uncertainty over tariffs. He also said there needs to be more demand rationing in the Canadian market.

The Canadian dollar was down less than one-tenth of a U.S. cent compared to Thursday's close.

About 33,300 contracts have traded at 10:07 CST.

 
Prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton:
